stories.title: Budget Guardrails
stories.description: When session cost approaches a user-defined threshold (default $5), automatically switch from expensive to cheaper model in the ProviderChain (e.g. claude-opus to claude-haiku), show a warning at 80% threshold, and pause for user approval at 100% threshold. Config key: budgetGuardrails: { warningAt: 4.00, hardLimit: 5.00, autoDowngrade: true } in .epam/settings.json.
acceptanceCriteria: budgetGuardrails config block (warningAt, hardLimit, autoDowngrade) is read from .epam/settings.json via ConfigResolver
BudgetGuard.ts tracks cumulative session cost in real-time by subscribing to cost events emitted after each LLM call
When session cost reaches warningAt, a yellow warning banner is shown in the REPL with current cost and limit
When session cost reaches hardLimit and autoDowngrade is true, ProviderChain automatically switches to the next cheaper model in the chain
When session cost reaches hardLimit and autoDowngrade is false, the REPL pauses and prompts user for approval before continuing
Model downgrade is logged to session JSONL with event type budget_downgrade, previous model, new model, and cost at switch
BudgetGuard exposes getCurrentCost() and getRemainingBudget() methods
Unit tests cover: warning threshold trigger, hard limit with autoDowngrade, hard limit without autoDowngrade, cost accumulation across multiple turns
Config type for budgetGuardrails added to ProjectConfig and ResolvedConfig in types.ts

acceptanceCriteria: Command `epam report` is registered in the Commander.js CLI and listed in `epam --help`
--client flag filters sessions by client name stored in session metadata
--format md produces a formatted markdown report; --format json produces a machine-readable JSON object
Report includes: total cost (USD), cost breakdown by model, total session count, average tokens per turn, and date range of sessions
SessionStore.listSessions() is updated to return parsed session metadata including model, tokenUsage, and cost fields
Report handles missing or malformed JSONL files gracefully with a warning line in output
Unit tests cover: markdown output format, JSON output format, filtering by client, empty session directory

stories.title: Agent Gold Profiles
stories.description: Implement the `epam profile [list|load|save|delete] [name]` command. Profiles are YAML or JSON files stored in ~/.epam/profiles/ or .epam/profiles/. Each profile has: name, description, systemPromptAppend, tools (enabled/disabled overrides), model, maxIterations, and tags. The `/profile [name]` slash command loads a profile into the current REPL session, merging its settings into the active AgentRunOptions.
acceptanceCriteria: ProfileStore reads from ~/.epam/profiles/ (global) and .epam/profiles/ (project-local, takes precedence)
Profile schema (name, description, systemPromptAppend, tools, model, maxIterations, tags) is defined in src/agent/profiles/types.ts
ProfileLoader merges profile into AgentRunOptions: systemPromptAppend is appended to base system prompt, tool overrides are applied, model and maxIterations override defaults if set
`epam profile list` prints all available profiles (global + local) with name, description, and tags
`epam profile save [name]` saves current session config as a profile YAML file
`epam profile load [name]` prints confirmation of what would be loaded (non-interactive mode)
`epam profile delete [name]` removes the profile file with confirmation prompt
SlashCommands.ts includes `/profile [name]` which calls ProfileLoader.load() and logs confirmation to REPL
Unit tests cover: list combining global+local, load/merge precedence, save serialization, delete confirmation

stories.title: /plan Mode
stories.description: Implement the `/plan` slash command that enters plan mode: the agent generates a step-by-step plan, presents it for user approval (Y/n/edit), then executes only after the user approves. The plan is stored as structured JSON with steps, affected files, and estimated complexity. Each step is shown in the REPL as it executes.
acceptanceCriteria: /plan slash command is registered in SlashCommands.ts and triggers PlanMode.generatePlan()
PlanMode.generatePlan() sends a structured planning prompt to the LLM and parses the response into a PlanStep[] JSON array
Each PlanStep has: stepNumber, description, affectedFiles (string[]), estimatedComplexity (low/medium/high)
The plan is displayed in the REPL with numbered steps, affected files, and complexity indicators using chalk formatting
User is prompted: 'Approve plan? [Y/n/edit]'. Y proceeds, n cancels, edit opens the plan JSON in $EDITOR
After approval, each step executes sequentially with a step counter header shown before each step
If a step fails, execution pauses and user is prompted to retry, skip, or abort
The approved plan JSON is stored in .epam/plans/ with a timestamped filename for later review
Unit tests cover: plan generation prompt structure, approval flow (Y/n/edit), step execution loop, failure pause

stories.title: Decision Records / Architectural DNA
stories.description: Implement the `epam decision [add|list|search]` command. Decisions are stored as JSONL in .epam/decisions.jsonl. Each record has: id, title, description, rationale, pattern_to_avoid, approved_alternative, tags, author, createdAt. A pre-call hook in AgentRunner loads relevant decisions and injects them into the system prompt before each LLM call.
acceptanceCriteria: Decision schema (id, title, description, rationale, pattern_to_avoid, approved_alternative, tags, author, createdAt) defined in src/decisions/types.ts
DecisionStore.add() appends a new record to .epam/decisions.jsonl with auto-generated id (DEC-001, DEC-002, etc.) and ISO createdAt timestamp
DecisionStore.list() returns all decisions parsed from JSONL
DecisionStore.search(query) performs case-insensitive keyword match across title, description, tags fields
`epam decision add` prompts for title, rationale, pattern_to_avoid, approved_alternative, tags via readline
`epam decision list` prints a formatted table of all decisions
`epam decision search [query]` prints matching decisions
ContextBuilder.ts injects the top 5 most relevant decisions as a '[DECISION RECORDS]' block in the system prompt before each agent call
Unit tests cover: add/list/search, injection into system prompt, empty decisions file gracefully handled

stories.title: MCP Client Mode
stories.description: Implement MCP client mode: read `.mcp.json` in the project root and register external MCP tool servers into the agent's tool registry at session start. Each server entry specifies name, url, and transport (http|sse). Remote tool calls are proxied through McpClient: serialise the call, send to the remote server, return the result to AgentRunner as if it were a local tool. Includes the `/mcp` slash command to list connected servers and their available tools.
acceptanceCriteria: McpClient.ts reads `.mcp.json` from project root (format: `{ servers: [{ name, url, transport }] }`) at session start
Each discovered remote server's tools are fetched via POST /mcp/tools/list and registered in the tool registry under a namespaced key `<server-name>/<tool-name>`
Remote tool calls are proxied: McpClient serialises the call as JSON-RPC 2.0, POSTs to the remote server, returns the result
If a remote server is unreachable at startup, a warning is logged and that server's tools are skipped — session continues
`.mcp.json` example file with one example server entry is included in the project root
Tool registry (src/tools/registry.ts) supports registering dynamically discovered MCP tools at runtime
`/mcp` slash command lists all configured MCP servers and their registered tools with connection status (connected/unreachable)
Unit tests cover: .mcp.json parsing, tool registration, remote tool proxy call, unreachable server skip

stories.title: /replay Command
stories.description: Implement `epam replay [session_id]` CLI command and `/replay` slash command. Reads a session JSONL file and replays each turn: shows user message, assistant response, and tool calls in sequence with a configurable delay. Supports --speed flag (1x/2x/4x). Useful for team leads to review agent reasoning.
acceptanceCriteria: `epam replay [session_id]` command is registered in Commander.js; without session_id, shows an interactive session picker using the prompts library
--speed flag accepts 1, 2, or 4; default is 1x; higher speeds reduce inter-turn delay
Each replayed turn displays: turn number, user message (cyan), assistant response (white), tool calls (yellow) with tool name and truncated args
Replay pauses at each turn for the speed-adjusted delay (1x=2s, 2x=1s, 4x=0.5s)
SessionStore.getSession(id) updated to return full parsed turn array from JSONL
`/replay` slash command in the REPL shows the interactive session picker and replays the chosen session inline
Replay gracefully handles malformed JSONL lines with a [SKIPPED: malformed turn] notice
Unit tests cover: speed flag parsing, turn rendering format, session picker logic, malformed line handling

`/sessions` slash command (distinct from `/replay`) opens an interactive session picker listing all saved sessions with ID, timestamp, and first user message; selecting an entry resumes that session via SessionStore.getSession(id) and loads its message history into the active REPL context
`/sessions` and `/replay` are registered as separate slash commands; `/sessions` switches context, `/replay` plays back without switching

stories.title: Shared Team Memory
stories.description: Implement `epam sync [push|pull|status]` command to sync .epam/context.md and .epam/decisions.jsonl to a shared backend endpoint (POST /v1/projects/{id}/context). Pull fetches the latest shared context. Requires auth token from KeychainKeyStore. Conflict resolution: detects diverged state (both local and remote changed since last sync) and blocks with a clear message. `--force` overrides and takes the specified side (local or remote). Last-write-wins only applies when there is no divergence.
acceptanceCriteria: `epam sync push` reads .epam/context.md and .epam/decisions.jsonl, POSTs to /v1/projects/{projectId}/context with Authorization header from auth token
`epam sync pull` fetches the remote context and decisions; writes to .epam/context.md and .epam/decisions.jsonl only if remote timestamp is newer
`epam sync status` shows: last push timestamp, last pull timestamp, local-vs-remote diff summary (lines added/removed)

`epam sync pull` detects a conflict when: (a) the remote timestamp is newer than last pull AND (b) local has been modified since last push. On conflict, command prints a diff summary and exits 1 with message: 'Conflict detected — local and remote have both changed since last sync. Use --force local or --force remote to resolve.'
BackendClient.ts updated with syncPush(projectId, payload) and syncPull(projectId) methods
ContextLoader.ts updated to check for a pulled remote context on startup and merge it with local context
Auth token is read from the existing KeychainKeyStore; if absent, command prints actionable error and exits 1
Unit tests cover: push payload structure, pull timestamp comparison (remote newer wins, local newer skips), status output, missing token error

`/memory` slash command in the REPL opens `.epam/context.md` (project-local) or `INSTRUCTIONS.md` in the user's `$EDITOR` (falling back to `nano`), then re-reads the file into ContextLoader on save so the updated instructions take effect immediately in the current session
`/memory --global` targets `~/.epam/config.md` (global context file) instead of the project-local file
`epam sync pull --force remote` overwrites local files with remote version unconditionally, logging: 'Overwrote local with remote (--force remote)'
`epam sync push --force` pushes local version unconditionally regardless of remote timestamp, logging: 'Force-pushed local over remote (--force)'
Last sync timestamps (push and pull) are persisted in `.epam/.sync-state.json` and used for divergence detection

stories.title: Ralph Wiggum Loop
stories.description: When a Bash tool call returns a non-zero exit code, spawn N parallel AgentRunner instances (default 3) each attempting a different fix strategy. Each instance receives the error output plus full context. The first instance to succeed wins and its output is used; the others are cancelled. Quality scoring prefers the fix with the lowest token complexity that also passes the original failing command on re-run.
acceptanceCriteria: RalphWiggumLoop.ts accepts: original command, stderr output, context messages, N (default 3), and a quality scorer function
N AgentRunner instances are spawned in parallel using Promise.race() with a cancellation token pattern
Each instance receives a unique fix strategy hint in its system prompt: 'Strategy 1: minimal change', 'Strategy 2: refactor approach', 'Strategy 3: alternative implementation'
After an instance produces a fix, the original failing Bash command is re-run to verify the fix actually works
Quality scoring ranks successful fixes by: (1) original command passes, (2) lowest total token count of fix diff
Losing instances are cancelled via AbortController signal passed to AgentRunner
AgentRunner.ts updated to call RalphWiggumLoop on recoverable Bash tool errors (non-zero exit with stderr)
Bash.ts updated to classify errors as recoverable (exit 1/2, stderr present) vs non-recoverable (SIGKILL, permission denied)
Unit tests cover: parallel spawn, first-success cancellation, quality scoring, non-recoverable error passthrough
stories.notes: Feature already implemented. Story moved to backlog for retroactive AC verification and documentation only — no new code required.

stories.title: Multi-Agent Squad Execution
stories.description: Implement `epam squad "task description"` command. Spawns a Leader agent that decomposes the task into subtasks, delegates to specialized sub-agents (Coder, Tester, SecurityAuditor) running in parallel, then runs a peer-review loop where the Auditor must approve the Coder's output before it is presented to the user. Uses AgentHandoff for delegation.
acceptanceCriteria: SquadRunner.ts orchestrates the full lifecycle: Leader → decompose → parallel Coder+Tester+SecurityAuditor → peer-review → present
Leader agent produces a SquadPlan JSON: { subtasks: [{ id, role, description, dependsOn }] }
Coder, Tester, SecurityAuditor roles defined in roles.ts with distinct system prompts and tool access
Coder and Tester run in parallel (Promise.all); SecurityAuditor runs after Coder completes
SecurityAuditor review: approves (proceed) or blocks (return to Coder with findings); max 2 re-review cycles
AgentHandoff.ts updated to support delegate_to_squad action type in addition to delegate_to_agent
`epam squad` command streams progress to terminal: [Leader] Decomposing... [Coder] Working... [SecurityAuditor] Reviewing...
Final output presented only after SecurityAuditor approval
Unit tests cover: decomposition parsing, parallel execution, Auditor block/approve cycle, max re-review limit

acceptanceCriteria: All EPAM-012 and EPAM-013 acceptance criteria are met and verified
Ralph Wiggum Loop never leaves unresolved Promise chains after the winner is selected
AbortController signals are properly propagated to all child AgentRunner instances
Squad Coder/Tester parallel execution does not share mutable state

stories.title: Remote Constraint Injection
stories.description: Implement a backend polling mechanism that calls GET /v1/projects/{id}/constraints at session start. The response contains active constraint rules that are injected into the system prompt. Each constraint has: id, rule, severity (warn/block), createdBy, expiresAt. Block-severity constraints are prepended prominently; warn-severity constraints are appended as notes.
acceptanceCriteria: ConstraintLoader fetches GET /v1/projects/{id}/constraints with auth token on AgentRunner session start
Constraint schema (id, rule, severity, createdBy, expiresAt) is defined and validated with zod
Expired constraints (expiresAt < now) are filtered out before injection
Block-severity constraints are injected at the top of the system prompt under a [CONSTRAINTS — MUST FOLLOW] header
Warn-severity constraints are injected at the bottom of the system prompt under a [ADVISORY CONSTRAINTS] header
If the endpoint is unreachable, a warning is logged but the session proceeds without constraints
Constraint fetch result is cached for the session duration (no re-fetch per turn)
Unit tests cover: constraint fetch, expiry filtering, severity separation in prompt, unreachable endpoint fallback

Trust model: constraint content is sourced exclusively from the EPAM platform-owned backend. No client-side sanitisation is applied to constraint rule text. This trust assumption is documented in a code comment in ConstraintLoader.ts: '// Trust: constraint content originates from the EPAM platform backend; prompt injection defence is enforced at the infrastructure layer.'

stories.title: Signature Auditor Agents
stories.description: Define named auditor personas (e.g. 'security-sarah', 'performance-paul') in .epam/auditors.json. After each agent response, the selected auditors run in parallel and produce critique transcripts. The user sees the debate — each auditor's findings — before the final answer is confirmed.
acceptanceCriteria: Auditor schema defined: { name, persona, focus, severity_threshold, model } in .epam/auditors.json
AuditorRegistry.ts reads .epam/auditors.json and instantiates auditor AgentRunner instances with persona-specific system prompts
After each primary agent response, enabled auditors run in parallel via Promise.all
Each auditor receives: the user message, the agent's proposed response, and the full conversation context
Auditor output is rendered in the REPL: '[AUDITOR: security-sarah] Finding: ...' in a distinct colour (magenta)
If any auditor raises a severity above the configured threshold (e.g. critical), the response is held and the user prompted to proceed or reject
Auditors can be toggled per-session via `/auditors [on|off|list]` slash command
Unit tests cover: parallel auditor execution, severity threshold gate, toggle on/off, persona injection

stories.title: Expertise Marketplace
stories.description: Implement `epam consult @profile-name` which pulls a named profile's systemPromptAppend and recent decision records into the current session context. Profiles are sourced from ~/.epam/profiles/ (implemented in EPAM-004). The consultation context is injected as a '[CONSULTING: @profile-name]' block in the next LLM call only.
acceptanceCriteria: `epam consult @profile-name` resolves the named profile via ProfileStore (from EPAM-004)
The profile's systemPromptAppend is injected as a '[CONSULTING: @profile-name]' block prepended to the next user message
The 3 most recently created decision records (from .epam/decisions.jsonl) tagged with matching profile tags are also injected
Consultation context applies only to the next single LLM call, then reverts to baseline session context
If profile does not exist, command prints error listing available profiles and exits 1
Consultation injection is visible in the REPL with a confirmation line: 'Consulting @profile-name for next response...'
Unit tests cover: profile resolution, single-turn context injection, tag-matched decision filtering, unknown profile error

stories.title: PRD Schema Validator
stories.description: Build orchestrations/scripts/lib/prd_validator.py — a standalone Python script that validates prd.json against a structural schema. Checks required fields on every story, referential integrity of dependency IDs and implementationOrder entries, enum constraints on priority/storyType/effort, no duplicate story IDs, and no orphaned stories. Uses Python stdlib only (json, sys, argparse). Exits 0 when valid, 1 when errors found. Produces a structured report listing each violation with story ID and field name.
acceptanceCriteria: File orchestrations/scripts/lib/prd_validator.py exists and is runnable with python3
python3 orchestrations/scripts/lib/prd_validator.py orchestrations/prd.json exits 0 and prints a summary line: 'N stories validated, 0 errors, K warnings'

Validates required fields on every story: id, title, description, priority, status, completed, agentGroup, agentRole, storyType, estimatedHours, dependencies, acceptanceCriteria — reports STORY_ID: missing field 'X' for each violation
All IDs in story.dependencies[] reference a story.id that exists in the stories array — reports 'STORY_ID: dependency DEP_ID not found'
All story IDs in implementationOrder phase arrays exist in the stories array — reports 'phase X: story ID Y not found in stories'
Every story.id in the stories array appears in exactly one implementationOrder phase — reports orphaned or duplicate-phase stories
No duplicate story IDs in the stories array — reports 'duplicate id: X'
priority validated against enum {low, medium, high, critical}
storyType validated against enum {implementation, review, health_check}
effort validated against enum {low, medium, high} when field is present
Flag --strict causes warnings (missing optional fields: humanHours, effort, cpaGate) to be treated as errors, exit 1
Flag --phase <id> scopes validation to stories in that phase only
Flag --json outputs violations as a JSON array instead of human-readable text
When intentionally given a prd.json with a missing required field on one story, script exits 1 and the error message identifies the story ID and field name
Pure Python stdlib — no third-party packages, no pip install required
Runs in under 2 seconds on a 30-story prd.json

stories.title: Context Guard — Agent File-Read Budget Enforcement
stories.description: Implement a ContextGuard module (sibling to BudgetGuard) that tracks cumulative tokens consumed by file-reading tools (ReadFile, Search, ListFiles, Bash output) during an agent session. When the running total approaches a configurable ceiling, warn the agent to narrow scope; when exceeded, block further file-read tool calls and inject a budget-exhausted message. Prevents runaway context loading where the agent reads the entire project for a narrow-scope task (observed: 974K cache_read_tokens for a single-file Python script in a TypeScript project). Configurable via maxContextTokens in project config, CLI flag --max-context-tokens, or EPAM_MAX_CONTEXT_TOKENS env var. Integrates at the Executor layer so all tool calls are metered without modifying individual tool implementations.
acceptanceCriteria: New ContextGuard class in src/agent/ tracks cumulative tokens read by file-reading tools (ReadFile, Search, ListFiles, Bash output) per session
maxContextTokens configurable via ResolvedConfig (default: 200000), CLI flag --max-context-tokens, and EPAM_MAX_CONTEXT_TOKENS env var
At 70% of budget: warning message injected into agent conversation advising it to narrow scope
At 100% of budget: file-read tools return a budget-exhausted error instead of file content; agent receives a message to work with existing context or request increase
Guard disabled when maxContextTokens is 0 or --no-context-guard flag is set
Executor integration — all tool results pass through ContextGuard token counting without changes to individual tool implementations
Token counting uses the same tokenizer approximation as BudgetGuard (1 token ≈ 4 chars)
Guard state (tokens consumed, files read, budget remaining) exposed via /context slash command in REPL
Orchestration integration: claude.sh can set EPAM_MAX_CONTEXT_TOKENS per story, scaled from technicalNotes.files scope
Unit tests cover: threshold warnings, hard block at 100%, disable via flag, token counting accuracy

stories.title: Orchestration Visual Report — draw.io + HTML Dashboard
stories.description: Generate a data-driven HTML report after each orchestration run that embeds draw.io diagrams inline alongside textual narrative, tables, and metrics. Diagrams are programmatically generated from execution data (prd.json, phase-cost.jsonl, cpa-review.jsonl, phase-gates.jsonl) using a draw.io MCP server. Report includes: (1) process flow diagram showing the orchestration pipeline (Estimation → CPA → Phase Gate → Execution → Assessment), (2) per-story swimlane showing each story journey with estimate-vs-actual annotations, (3) estimation accuracy comparison (Formula vs CPA-blended vs Actual). Delivered as a single-page HTML file per phase with draw.io JS viewer for embedded diagrams. Can be triggered post-execution automatically or on-demand via CLI command (e.g. epam report --phase <id>).
acceptanceCriteria: HTML report generated per phase containing embedded draw.io diagrams and textual narrative
Process flow diagram shows orchestration pipeline stages (Estimation, CPA, Phase Gate, Execution, Assessment) with status indicators
Per-story swimlane diagram shows each story progression through pipeline with estimate-vs-actual annotations on time, cost, turns
Estimation accuracy section shows Formula vs CPA-blended vs Actual as a visual comparison per story
Diagrams are programmatically generated from prd.json, phase-cost.jsonl, cpa-review.jsonl, and phase-gates.jsonl — not manually drawn
draw.io XML embedded inline in HTML using draw.io JS viewer (no external .drawio files)
Textual sections include phase summary, per-story metrics table, gate decision, and assessment findings
Report can be triggered automatically post-orchestration or on-demand via epam report --phase <id>
Report renders correctly when opened in any modern browser without a server
draw.io MCP server integration for programmatic diagram generation

stories.title: Human Phase Approval Gate in epam-cli

stories.title: Model Registry and Selection (Provider: epam)

stories.title: Board Visualization — Provider/Model by Story

stories.title: Provider Cutover Policy — OpenCode to epam
stories.description: Add a controlled provider cutover policy that can safely migrate pending stories from OpenCode to epam when readiness preconditions are met. stories.title: /permissions Slash Command
stories.description: Implement the `/permissions` slash command in the REPL that displays the current tool approval policy and allows the user to toggle it without restarting the session. Shows which tools are auto-approved, which require confirmation, and which are disabled. Surfaces the existing ToolRunner approval configuration interactively.
acceptanceCriteria: `/permissions` is registered in SlashCommands.ts and appears in `/help` output
Output shows a table of all registered tools with columns: tool name, safety tier (safe/review/dangerous), current approval mode (auto/prompt/disabled)
`/permissions auto` sets all tools to auto-approve for the remainder of the session (equivalent to `EPAM_DANGEROUS_SKIP_APPROVAL=1`)

stories.title: /diff Slash Command
stories.description: Implement the `/diff` slash command in the REPL that runs `git diff` (staged + unstaged) and `git status` in the current project root, displaying the output inline with syntax highlighting. Provides a quick way to review what the agent has changed mid-session without leaving the chat.
acceptanceCriteria: `/diff` is registered in SlashCommands.ts and appears in `/help` output
`/diff` runs `git diff HEAD` and `git status --short` in `process.cwd()`, streaming output to the REPL with ANSI colour preserved (green additions, red removals)
`/diff --staged` runs `git diff --staged` only
`/diff [path]` scopes the diff to a specific file or directory
If not inside a git repository, prints an informative error and exits gracefully (no throw)
Output is paginated: if diff exceeds 100 lines, shows first 100 lines and prints `[truncated — N more lines]`
Unit tests cover: git-not-found error, no-changes output, staged flag, path scoping

stories.title: /debug Slash Command
stories.description: Implement the `/debug` slash command in the REPL that dumps session diagnostics inline: current config (provider, model, approval policy), active context size, tool registry, provider health, last N error events, and environment variable overrides. Mirrors the functionality of `epam doctor` but scoped to the running session without requiring a restart.
acceptanceCriteria: `/debug` is registered in SlashCommands.ts and appears in `/help` output

Output sections: Session Config (provider, model, maxIterations, approval policy), Context Window (tokens used, % full, message count), Tool Registry (name, tier, approval mode for each registered tool), Provider Health (slot, state, last error, retry-after), Env Overrides (any EPAM_* env vars set, values redacted for secrets)
`/debug --json` emits the same data as a single JSON object to stdout (useful for piping)
Secrets (API keys, JWT tokens) are redacted to `[REDACTED]` in all output modes

stories.title: /fork Slash Command
stories.description: Implement the `/fork` slash command in the REPL that clones the current conversation state into a new parallel session, allowing the user to explore an alternative solution path without losing the original thread. The forked session is saved under a new session ID and the user is shown both session IDs for later `/resume`.
acceptanceCriteria: `/fork` is registered in SlashCommands.ts and appears in `/help` output

`/fork` deep-copies the current session's message history into a new JSONL session file under a new UUID, writes it via SessionStore.saveSession(), and prints: `Forked → session <new-id>. Current session: <original-id>. Use /resume <id> to switch.`
`/fork [label]` optionally tags the forked session with a human-readable label stored in session metadata
The original session continues unaffected after a fork
The forked session appears in `/sessions` picker with a 🔀 fork indicator and reference to origin session ID

stories.title: /tasks Slash Command
stories.description: Implement the `/tasks` slash command in the REPL that lists and manages background async tasks spawned during the current session (e.g. parallel squad sub-agents from EPAM-013, background tool executions). Shows task ID, status, elapsed time, and a truncated description. Allows the user to cancel or await a specific task.
acceptanceCriteria: `/tasks` is registered in SlashCommands.ts and appears in `/help` output

`/tasks` prints a table of all tasks in the current session: ID (short), status with colour coding (🟡 running, ✅ done, ❌ failed, ⛔ cancelled), elapsed seconds, truncated description

stories.title: /vim Slash Command
stories.description: Implement the `/vim` slash command in the REPL that toggles vim-style keybindings for the readline input composer. When enabled, the input line supports normal/insert mode switching (Esc/i), navigation (h/j/k/l, w/b, 0/$), and basic editing operators (d, c, x, y, p). Persists preference to `.epam/settings.json`.
acceptanceCriteria: `/vim` is registered in SlashCommands.ts and toggles vim mode on/off, printing current state: `Vim mode: ON` or `Vim mode: OFF`

When vim mode is ON, input readline switches to vi-mode (using readline's built-in vi-mode or a readline-vim library), supporting: Esc (normal mode), i/a/A/I/o/O (insert mode), h/l/w/b/0/$ (motion), x/dd/dw (delete), yy/p (yank/paste)
Mode indicator shows in the prompt prefix: `[N]` for normal mode, `[I]` for insert mode
`/vim` preference is persisted in `.epam/settings.json` under `repl.vimMode: true/false` and restored on next session start
Unit tests cover: toggle on/off, settings persistence, mode indicator rendering

stories.title: /editor Slash Command
stories.description: Implement the `/editor` slash command in the REPL that opens the user's `$EDITOR` (defaulting to `nano`) with a temporary file, allowing composition of a long multi-line prompt. On save-and-exit, the file contents are sent as the next user message. Useful for complex, multi-paragraph prompts that are awkward to type inline.
acceptanceCriteria: `/editor` is registered in SlashCommands.ts and appears in `/help` output
Opens `$EDITOR` (falling back to `$VISUAL`, then `nano`, then `vi`) with a temp file at `/tmp/epam-prompt-<uuid>.md`; REPL input is suspended while the editor is open
On editor exit with a non-empty file, the file contents are submitted as the next user turn exactly as if typed
On editor exit with an empty file or no changes, prints `[Editor closed with no content — message cancelled]` and returns to the prompt
Temp file is deleted after submission or cancellation
`/editor --template <name>` pre-populates the temp file with a named template from `.epam/prompt-templates/`
Unit tests cover: successful submission, empty-file cancellation, template injection, temp file cleanup

stories.title: /init Slash Command — Project Scaffolding
stories.description: Implement the `/init` slash command in the REPL (and `epam init` as a top-level CLI command) that scaffolds a new EPAM CLI project. Creates `.epam/settings.json` from a built-in defaults template and `INSTRUCTIONS.md` from a built-in markdown template if either file is absent. Prints a per-file confirmation (created / already exists / skipped). Idempotent: safe to run on an existing project.

Both entry points share the same underlying ScaffoldRunner.ts implementation
ScaffoldRunner checks for `.epam/settings.json`; if absent, writes it from `src/templates/settings.default.json` with sensible defaults (provider, model, budgetGuardrails, tools)
ScaffoldRunner checks for `INSTRUCTIONS.md`; if absent, writes it from `src/templates/INSTRUCTIONS.default.md` containing placeholder sections: Project Context, Coding Standards, Out of Scope
If `.epam/` directory does not exist, it is created automatically
Each file outcome is printed: `✔ created .epam/settings.json`, `⚠ skipped INSTRUCTIONS.md (already exists)`
Running `/init` or `epam init` on an already-initialised project makes no changes and exits 0
Templates live in `src/templates/` and are bundled into the tsup build output

stories.title: MCP Server Mode
stories.description: Expose epam-cli as an MCP server so other agents and Claude instances can call epam-cli's built-in tools (ReadFile, WriteFile, Bash, ListFiles, Search, FetchUrl) via JSON-RPC 2.0 over HTTP and SSE. Command: `epam mcp serve [--port 3100]`. Requires EPAM-007 (MCP Client) to be complete first as it establishes the shared MCP types and tool registry patterns.
acceptanceCriteria: `epam mcp serve [--port 3100]` starts an HTTP server exposing MCP endpoints: POST /mcp/tools/list, POST /mcp/tools/call, GET /mcp/sse
McpServer.ts implements JSON-RPC 2.0 dispatch for all six built-in tools (ReadFile, WriteFile, Bash, ListFiles, Search, FetchUrl)
SSE endpoint (GET /mcp/sse) streams tool call progress events to connected clients in the MCP event format
All tool calls through McpServer respect the same approval policy as local tool calls (dangerous tools require explicit server-side approval config)

Graceful shutdown: SIGTERM/SIGINT closes all SSE connections before exit
`epam mcp serve --list-tools` prints available tools without starting the server (dry-run introspection)
Unit tests cover: tool list endpoint response, tool call dispatch for each built-in tool, invalid JSON-RPC error response, SSE connection lifecycle

stories.title: MEMORY.md Auto-Loading System
stories.description: Implement the core memory file loader that discovers and injects MEMORY.md files into every agent session at startup. Loads a four-level hierarchy: (1) global `~/.epam/MEMORY.md`, (2) project `.epam/MEMORY.md`, (3) machine-local `.epam/MEMORY.local.md` (gitignored, never synced), (4) `INSTRUCTIONS.md` in project root (backward-compat alias). Each file is injected as a labelled context block in the system prompt. Supports `@path/to/file` import syntax (max 3 levels deep). Enforces a 200-line soft limit per file — files exceeding this print a startup warning but still load. Re-reads and re-injects all files when `/compact` runs.
acceptanceCriteria: MemoryLoader.ts discovers files in load order: `~/.epam/MEMORY.md` → `.epam/MEMORY.md` → `.epam/MEMORY.local.md` → `INSTRUCTIONS.md`
Each loaded file is injected into the system prompt as a clearly labelled block: `[GLOBAL MEMORY]`, `[PROJECT MEMORY]`, `[LOCAL MEMORY]`, `[INSTRUCTIONS]`
Files that do not exist are silently skipped with no error
`@path/to/file` import syntax: references relative to the containing MEMORY.md file, resolved recursively up to 3 hops; broken imports print a startup warning
Files exceeding 200 lines print: `⚠ ~/.epam/MEMORY.md exceeds 200 lines — consider pruning for best results` but still load in full
`.epam/MEMORY.local.md` is added to `.gitignore` on first creation (same behaviour as Claude Code's CLAUDE.local.md)
On `/compact`, MemoryLoader.reloadAll() is called and fresh file contents replace the previous memory blocks in the compacted context
Total injected memory token count is tracked and exposed via `MemoryLoader.totalTokens()`
Unit tests cover: all four files present, partial presence, import resolution, circular import guard (max 3 hops), 200-line warning, compact re-inject

stories.title: TEAM-MEMORY.md — Shared Team Context
stories.description: Implement `.epam/TEAM-MEMORY.md` as a fifth memory tier specifically for shared team knowledge. Unlike personal MEMORY.md (which records individual preferences and project notes), TEAM-MEMORY.md is append-only and entry-stamped: every addition carries an author identity and ISO timestamp. It is injected as a `[TEAM MEMORY]` block after project memory and before local overrides. The append-only model prevents one team member from silently overwriting another's entries. Integrates with EPAM-010 (`epam sync`) as a dedicated sync target — TEAM-MEMORY.md is always synced on push/pull alongside context.md. Conflict resolution for TEAM-MEMORY: remote entries are merged in (union of entries by timestamp), never overwritten — no force flag needed since entries are additive.
acceptanceCriteria: `.epam/TEAM-MEMORY.md` is loaded by MemoryLoader as the `[TEAM MEMORY]` block, injected after `[PROJECT MEMORY]` and before `[LOCAL MEMORY]`
Entry format enforced on append: `<!-- [ISO-TIMESTAMP] [AUTHOR] -->\n[content]\n`; free-form sections outside this format are loaded as-is (backward compat)
`epam sync push` and `epam sync pull` include TEAM-MEMORY.md as a sync target alongside context.md
TEAM-MEMORY sync uses union-merge: on pull, remote entries with timestamps not present locally are appended; no remote entry ever overwrites a local entry
Merge produces a chronologically sorted TEAM-MEMORY.md after each pull
`epam sync status` reports TEAM-MEMORY separately: `TEAM-MEMORY: N local-only, M remote-only, K shared entries`
`.epam/TEAM-MEMORY.md` is committed to git (not gitignored) — it is the canonical team knowledge base

200-line soft limit applies to the injected portion; if TEAM-MEMORY exceeds 200 lines, only the 200 most recent entries (by timestamp) are injected, with a note: `[TEAM MEMORY truncated — N entries omitted, use 'epam memory show --team' to view all]`
Unit tests cover: load order, entry append format, pull merge (union semantics), chronological sort, 200-line truncation to most-recent

stories.title: `epam memory` CLI Commands
stories.description: Implement `epam memory [show|edit|append|clear]` top-level CLI command suite for managing all memory tiers. Provides the same capability as Claude Code's `/memory` slash command but also as a top-level CLI command usable outside the REPL (e.g. from scripts, CI, or `--print` mode). The `/memory` slash command already has an AC in EPAM-010; this story delivers the full implementation with all subcommands and the REPL integration upgrade.
acceptanceCriteria: `epam memory show` prints all loaded memory tiers in order: global, project, team, local, instructions — each with file path, line count, and first 3 lines preview
`epam memory show --global|--project|--team|--local` prints only the specified tier in full
`epam memory edit [--global|--project|--team|--local]` opens the target file in `$EDITOR` (default: nano); creates the file from a template if it does not exist
`epam memory append [--global|--project|--team] "<text>"` appends text to the target memory file; for --team, wraps in the entry format with author+timestamp automatically
`epam memory clear --local` deletes `.epam/MEMORY.local.md` with confirmation prompt; other tiers require `--confirm` flag to prevent accidental deletion
`/memory` slash command in REPL is upgraded to match: shows loaded tiers, offers edit/append inline, reflects any appends immediately in the current session (hot-apply for the running session only)
`epam memory show --stats` prints: total lines loaded, total tokens consumed by memory, % of context window used by memory
Unit tests cover: show formatting, edit file creation, append team entry format, clear confirmation guard, slash command hot-apply

stories.title: Memory Compaction Re-injection and Hot-Apply
stories.description: Ensure memory files survive `/compact` correctly — re-reading from disk and re-injecting fresh content into the compacted context, exactly as Claude Code re-injects CLAUDE.md after compaction. Also implements hot-apply: when `epam memory append` or `/memory append` adds content during a session, the new content is applied to the running session's context without requiring a restart. Adds a `--reload` flag to the `/memory` command to force re-read all files mid-session.
acceptanceCriteria: When `/compact` runs, `MemoryLoader.reloadAll()` is called after compaction and fresh memory blocks are prepended to the compacted summary before the next turn
Memory content visible after compaction matches current on-disk files — edits made between session start and compact are reflected
`/memory reload` (or `/memory --reload`) force-reads all memory files and applies them to the current session context immediately, printing: `Memory reloaded: N lines injected from X files`

When `epam memory append` is called from the CLI while a REPL session is active, the REPL detects the file change (via fs.watch on memory files) and prints: `[Memory updated — run /memory reload to apply]`
`MemoryLoader` exposes a `watch(callback)` method that fires when any tracked memory file changes on disk
Unit tests cover: compact re-inject, hot-apply after append, fs.watch callback, reload summary output

stories.title: Provider Authentication Competitive Baseline
stories.description: Document the current provider-authentication requirements and UX patterns for Anthropic Claude Code, OpenCode, Cursor CLI, and OpenAI Codex, then convert the findings into an EPAM CLI auth architecture decision. The goal is to define the temporary bridge model for v1 while preserving the long-term direction that `epam login` provisions and refreshes provider credentials centrally.
acceptanceCriteria: A research note is produced comparing Anthropic Claude Code, OpenCode, Cursor CLI, and OpenAI Codex auth models with source links and capture date
The note distinguishes provider-native browser login, organization/workspace-scoped API keys, manually entered API keys, and auto-generated brokered keys
Anthropic findings explicitly cover Claude.ai subscription login, Anthropic Console login, and workspace-managed key constraints
OpenCode findings explicitly cover `opencode auth login`, credential storage location, Anthropic Claude Pro/Max flow, and OpenAI ChatGPT Plus/Pro flow
Cursor findings explicitly cover browser login, API-key fallback, user API keys, and the limitation that custom keys only power standard chat models
OpenAI Codex findings explicitly cover `codex --login`, Sign in with ChatGPT, and the auto-generated API-key model
A short ADR is added under `.epam/decisions.jsonl` or equivalent project decision storage defining EPAM CLI provider-auth direction for v1 and non-goals
The architecture note identifies which flows can be implemented directly by EPAM CLI versus which depend on provider-supported browser/device/OAuth mechanics
Unit-test impact and security review checkpoints are listed for downstream implementation stories

The architecture note explicitly distinguishes the short-term bridge approach from the long-term EPAM-brokered local credential model where `epam login` provisions provider access and stores refreshable credentials on the developer machine

stories.title: Bridge Credential Broker and Storage Abstraction
stories.description: Introduce a credential subsystem that supports both today's manual/provider-native bridge flows and the long-term EPAM-brokered local model. This separates EPAM identity (`epam login`) from temporary provider identity workarounds while ensuring the storage and resolution layer can later accept provider credentials provisioned by the EPAM platform and stored securely on the machine.
acceptanceCriteria: A new provider credential model is defined with credential types for `api_key`, `browser_session`, and `brokered_key`
Credential metadata includes provider, account label, workspace or organization label when available, createdAt, expiresAt, and refreshability flags
Credential storage reuses secure local persistence patterns already used by token storage and key storage, with masked inspection output only

A provider-credential resolver chooses the best available credential source in deterministic priority order, with clear precedence for future EPAM-brokered local credentials over temporary provider-native bridge credentials
Existing `epam keys` BYOK behavior continues to work unchanged for manual API-key users
ProviderChain and config resolution are updated so provider-native credentials can satisfy Anthropic or OpenAI provider requests without raw environment variables
Credential schema supports future provider-specific tokens or exchanged API keys returned by browser auth flows
Unit tests cover secure persistence, provider selection precedence, expired credential rejection, and backward compatibility with stored BYOK keys
Credential schema includes a source field that distinguishes `epam_brokered_local`, `provider_browser`, and `manual_api_key`

stories.title: SDK Utility: formatTokenCount — Human-Readable Token Count Formatter
stories.description: Implement and fully test a pure utility function `formatTokenCount(n: number): string` that converts raw integer token counts into compact, human-readable display strings for use in billing output and the cost-tracking dashboard. The function must handle the sub-thousand, K (thousands), and M (millions) ranges, apply consistent one-decimal rounding, and be resilient to edge-case numeric inputs.
acceptanceCriteria: formatTokenCount(0) returns '0'
formatTokenCount(0.0) returns '0' (IEEE 754 positive zero — treated as the integer 0, not rejected as a float)
formatTokenCount(1) returns '1'
formatTokenCount(999) returns '999'
formatTokenCount(1000) returns '1.0K'
formatTokenCount(1500) returns '1.5K'
formatTokenCount(1999) returns '2.0K' (rounds half-up to one decimal place at the K boundary)
formatTokenCount(2000) returns '2.0K' (exact K boundary with no fractional digits needed)
formatTokenCount(2550) returns '2.6K' (rounds half-up to one decimal place within K range)
formatTokenCount(999999) returns '999.9K' — values >= 1000 and < 1000000 always use the K suffix; the M suffix is reserved exclusively for values >= 1000000
formatTokenCount(1000000) returns '1.0M'
formatTokenCount(1500000) returns '1.5M'
formatTokenCount(2000000) returns '2.0M' (exact M boundary with no fractional digits needed)
formatTokenCount(2500000) returns '2.5M'
formatTokenCount(2550000) returns '2.6M' (rounds half-up to one decimal place within M range)
formatTokenCount(999999999) returns '1000.0M' — no B/G suffix is required; values beyond the M range remain expressed in M

Calling formatTokenCount with a negative number (e.g. -1) throws a RangeError — the thrown value must be an instance of RangeError — with message exactly equal to 'formatTokenCount: n must be a non-negative finite integer'

Calling formatTokenCount with negative zero (-0) throws a RangeError with message exactly equal to 'formatTokenCount: n must be a non-negative finite integer' (negative zero is not a valid token count)
Calling formatTokenCount with NaN throws a RangeError — instance of RangeError — with message exactly equal to 'formatTokenCount: n must be a non-negative finite integer'
Calling formatTokenCount with Infinity throws a RangeError — instance of RangeError — with message exactly equal to 'formatTokenCount: n must be a non-negative finite integer'
Calling formatTokenCount with -Infinity throws a RangeError — instance of RangeError — with message exactly equal to 'formatTokenCount: n must be a non-negative finite integer'

Calling formatTokenCount with a non-integer finite positive number (e.g. 1500.7) throws a RangeError — instance of RangeError — with message exactly equal to 'formatTokenCount: n must be a non-negative finite integer'

The decimal separator in all returned strings is the ASCII period character U+002E regardless of the Node.js process locale or any ICU locale setting; the function must not use any Intl or locale-sensitive formatting API
The function is pure: it reads only its single argument, produces a return value, and causes no observable side effects (no I/O, no mutation of module-level or global state, no console output)
The function signature is exactly: export function formatTokenCount(n: number): string
The function is the sole named export from src/utils/formatTokenCount.ts; the module has no default export
The module contains no runtime dependencies beyond the TypeScript standard library (no package.json imports, no Node built-ins beyond what the TS lib provides for number operations)
Vitest unit tests reside at test/unit/utils/formatTokenCount.test.ts and import the function as a named import from src/utils/formatTokenCount.ts

The test file organises cases using describe/it or describe/test blocks grouped at minimum by: (a) sub-thousand values, (b) K-range values including rounding, (c) M-range values including rounding, (d) invalid-input error cases — making test intent auditable without running them

Test file covers: zero (0 and 0.0), sub-thousand boundary (1, 999), exact K boundaries (1000, 2000), mid-K value (1500), rounding within K range (1999 → '2.0K', 2550 → '2.6K'), K ceiling (999999 → '999.9K'), exact M boundaries (1000000, 2000000), mid-M value (1500000), rounding within M range (2550000 → '2.6M'), large M value (999999999 → '1000.0M'), and all six invalid-input error cases (negative integer, negative zero, NaN, Infinity, -Infinity, non-integer positive float)
Each invalid-input test asserts both that the thrown object is an instance of RangeError and that its message property equals 'formatTokenCount: n must be a non-negative finite integer'
All Vitest tests pass with `vitest run` and no tests are marked skip, todo, or only
TypeScript compilation of both src/utils/formatTokenCount.ts and test/unit/utils/formatTokenCount.test.ts produces zero errors under the project's tsconfig

Story is simple in scope but used in billing/cost-tracking contexts (financial accuracy matters), so both agents are warranted. openspec should elaborate edge cases (negative numbers, NaN, Infinity, non-integer inputs, rounding behavior at boundaries like 1050, 999999) and clarify locale/decimal separator assumptions. speckit should then harden testability criteria, flag missing boundary guards, and ensure security/correctness of numeric formatting in a billing context.

agentContributions.notes: Openspec produced a thorough, well-structured set of AC. No criteria were removed. Changes made: (1) Flagged the 999999 boundary AC for human disambiguation — the AC resolves the ambiguity correctly in prose but the leading sentence still contains 'OR' which could confuse an automated test generator. (2) Added AC for the float-zero case (0.0), Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER guard behaviour, and negative-zero (-0) edge case — all legitimate numeric inputs a TypeScript caller could pass. (3) Added AC requiring the error thrown is strictly a RangeError instance (not just any Error), enabling instanceof assertions in tests. (4) Added AC for exact two-thousand K boundary (2000 → '2.0K') which was mentioned in the test-coverage list but not in the assertion list, creating a gap between what tests are expected to cover and what is formally specified. (5) Added AC for the rounding case 2550 → '2.6K' in the K range which likewise appeared only in the test-coverage list without a top-level AC. (6) Added AC for exact M boundary 2000000 → '2.0M', same gap as above. (7) Added AC for locale-independence: the formatted string must use a literal ASCII period as the decimal separator regardless of runtime locale, since toFixed() is locale-safe but Intl-based alternatives are not — clarifying the implementation constraint. (8) Added AC that the test file uses describe/it (or describe/test) grouping by input category, making the file structure auditable. (9) Added AC that the function is pure (no side effects, no I/O, no mutation of external state) — required for billing-grade reliability and testability without mocking.

coordinatorReview.reviewNotes: Both agents contributed meaningfully and non-overlappingly across distinct layers of the spec. openspec addressed the core gaps in the original 8-item AC set: input validation guards (negative, NaN, Infinity, non-integer), rounding semantics, export shape, and test structure scaffolding — transforming an underspecified stub into a workable spec. speckit then added a distinct, additive layer: resolving the ambiguous '999999 → 1000.0K OR 1.0M' OR-clause into the unambiguous '999.9K' assertion, adding IEEE 754 edge cases (0.0 positive zero, -0 negative zero), upgrading loose error message checks to strict instanceof+message pair assertions, splitting the combined 'Infinity or -Infinity' AC into individually verifiable assertions, adding the locale/decimal-separator constraint (critical for billing correctness), the function purity AC, and test-file structural organisation requirements. There is no meaningful AC overlap between agent contributions — each built on the prior layer without duplicating it. The final 34-item AC set is cohesive, fully testable, unambiguous, and appropriately scoped for a billing-grade utility. No story split is warranted; the scope is correctly self-contained. Two minor concerns are carried forward from the embedded coordinator review and are confirmed here: (1) speckit's agent notes reference a 'Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER guard behaviour' AC that was considered but intentionally omitted — the 999999999 proxy case is a reasonable substitute for most billing scenarios but MAX_SAFE_INTEGER+1 (9007199254740992) introduces IEEE 754 precision ambiguity where Number.isInteger() returns true but the value is not representable as a distinct integer, which could produce silent correctness failures in extreme billing edge cases. Human reviewer should decide whether an explicit upper-bound cap AC is needed. (2) The 'no tests marked skip, todo, or only' upgrade from speckit (vs openspec's 'no tests are skipped') is a meaningful improvement — 'only' in particular can cause silent CI gaps that the original phrasing would not catch. This upgrade is noted positively. Overall spec quality is high.
flags: MINOR: Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER+1 (9007199254740992) edge case not covered — Number.isInteger() returns true for this value but IEEE 754 double precision cannot distinguish it from adjacent integers; confirm whether an explicit upper-bound cap AC is needed for billing safety or whether the existing non-integer guard is considered sufficient

MINOR: speckit agent notes explicitly reference a 'Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER guard behaviour' AC that was evaluated but not finalised — recommend human reviewer explicitly sign off on the intentional omission to avoid the gap re-surfacing silently in implementation

INFORMATIONAL: negative zero (-0) AC correctly throws RangeError, but the implementation detail of detecting -0 (Object.is(n, -0) vs n < 0) is not constrained by any AC — implementation is free to use any correct mechanism; no action needed unless implementation guidance is desired
